title: SFI Professors Give Judges Advice on AI
gist: At New Mexico's National Judicial Summit, SFI researchers Cristopher Moore, Melanie Mitchell, and Melanie Moses warned roughly 300 judges that AI now touches every stage of criminal justice — from facial recognition to legal writing to a documented 69% jump in unrepresented Fair Housing Act filings — and urged treating opaque AI evidence with the same skepticism as an anonymous tip, since proprietary black-box systems undermine defendants' right to confront their accusers.

insights.claim: Artificial intelligence now touches every stage of a criminal case, from the police investigation through the trial, making the operative question for judges not whether to use it but how to see inside it.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Frames AI adoption in the justice system as already pervasive and irreversible, shifting the policy debate from gatekeeping to auditability and transparency.
insights.quote: AI now touches every stage of a criminal case; SFI researchers told judges the question is not whether to use it, but how to see inside it.

insights.claim: SFI Resident Professors Cristopher Moore and Melanie Mitchell and External Professor Melanie Moses addressed AI's implications for criminal justice at the National Judicial Summit on the Foundation and Future of the Judiciary in Santa Fe, attended by roughly 300 judges, two-thirds from New Mexico and the rest from 22 states and eight tribal nations.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Indicates SFI is actively shaping how a broad, multi-state judicial audience thinks about deploying and scrutinizing AI in courts — a channel for real institutional influence on AI governance in the legal system.

insights.claim: Berkeley law professor Andrea Roth and Maryland public defender Marc Canellas, both experts on AI-generated evidence, also participated in the panels after having attended a 2025 SFI working group on AI and Justice supported by the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation through SFI's Emergent Engineering project.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Shows SFI is building a sustained, funded pipeline (working group to summit) connecting complexity researchers with practicing legal experts on AI evidence.
insights.locator: Two guests of SFI also participated in the panel discussions

insights.claim: AI has made facial-recognition technology much more effective for police, but Moore argues it should be treated as an anonymous tip rather than grounds for arrest because it is far from infallible.
insights.speaker: Cristopher Moore
insights.so_what: A concrete evidentiary standard investors/policymakers tracking AI-in-law-enforcement regulation should watch for: courts or legislatures codifying 'tip, not proof' treatment would materially change facial-recognition vendors' liability and adoption model.
insights.quote: Do we want cameras on every street corner that are constantly watching us?
insights.locator: For example, AI has made facial-recognition technology much more effective

insights.claim: Facial recognition and other AI tools may perpetuate biases against certain demographic groups because their training data underrepresents vulnerable groups, though whether AI is more biased than humans remains an open academic question according to Moses.
insights.speaker: Melanie Moses
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the simple 'AI is more biased than humans' narrative — bias comparisons need rigorous study, not assumption, before informing policy.
insights.locator: Facial recognition and other AI tools may also perpetuate biases

themes.theme: Hallucination risk in AI-assisted legal writing

insights.claim: AI expedites legal writing but risks hallucinations, and some lawyers and judges have already been embarrassed when their AI-assisted briefs were found to contain fictional cases or misinterpretations of real cases.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Documented real-world failure mode already generating professional embarrassment for lawyers/judges — a live liability and malpractice risk in legal-AI tooling.

insights.claim: As AI legal-writing tools improve, the mistakes they produce become more subtle and harder to spot, according to Moses.
insights.speaker: Melanie Moses
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive risk trajectory: better models don't eliminate hallucination risk in high-stakes review workflows, they make it harder to catch — argues for durable demand in AI-output verification tooling rather than assuming the problem self-resolves with model quality.
insights.quote: as these tools get better, the mistakes become more subtle and harder to spot

themes.theme: AI reshaping access to the justice system

insights.claim: Chatbots now let laypeople assess whether their case is worth bringing before a judge, and as a result the number of lawsuits filed without a lawyer has jumped substantially.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: A structural shift in court caseloads driven by AI legal-advice access — implies rising demand on court administrative capacity and a new class of AI-adjacent legal-tech use cases (pro se assistance).
insights.locator: Laypeople can now ask chatbots whether their case is worth bringing

insights.claim: Federal courts saw a 69% increase in Fair Housing Act cases filed without a lawyer in the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Concrete, citable metric quantifying the AI-driven pro se litigation surge in a specific case category — a useful leading indicator for court-system strain and self-help legal-tech demand.
insights.locator: federal courts saw a 69% increase in cases related to the Fair Housing Act

insights.claim: AI's tendency toward sycophancy means it sometimes erroneously tells users they have a good case, and judges will have to adjust to how AI is shifting the nature of cases that reach their courtrooms, according to Moses.
insights.speaker: Melanie Moses
insights.so_what: Sycophancy bias in consumer-facing legal chatbots directly degrades case quality entering the court system — a specific, measurable AI-safety failure mode with downstream institutional cost.
insights.locator: AI, with its tendency toward sycophancy

themes.theme: Transparency and the black-box evidence problem

insights.claim: Judges attending the summit ranged from enthusiastic to highly skeptical about AI, mirroring the range of views in the general population, according to Moore.
insights.speaker: Cristopher Moore
insights.so_what: No institutional consensus yet exists among judges on AI — regulatory and evidentiary standards will likely emerge unevenly across jurisdictions rather than uniformly.
insights.locator: Judges who attended the sessions had a wide range of mindsets

insights.claim: Moore argues AI can help deliver justice only if the tools are designed to be transparent, since some AI evidence systems are proprietary and prevent independent testing for accuracy and fairness, making it hard or impossible for the defense to contest the evidence.
insights.speaker: Cristopher Moore
insights.so_what: A direct constitutional friction point (confrontation clause) that will likely drive future litigation and regulation forcing transparency/auditability requirements on AI evidence vendors used by law enforcement and courts.
insights.quote: We have a constitutional right to confront our accusers, and you can't confront a black box.
insights.locator: In particular, one message Moore was sure to convey
